objectives: Describe and distinguish static and dynamic data structures, including arrays, records, lists, tuples and linked lists.
Trace operations on stacks and queues, and explain their LIFO/FIFO behaviour and real-world uses.
Explain how graphs, trees, binary search trees and hash tables store and retrieve data, including traversal order and collisions.

retrieval.q: Which data structure operates on a Last In, First Out basis?
retrieval.a: A stack (push and pop at the top).

retrieval.q: Which BST traversal visits nodes in ascending order?
What is a collision in a hash table?
retrieval.a: When two different keys hash to the same index.

retrieval.q: Interleaving (1.4.1): What is the denary value of the unsigned binary 00101101?
Interleaving (1.4.3): Give the Boolean expression for the Sum output of a half adder.

keywords.def: An ordered, fixed-size collection of items of the same type accessed by a numeric index, giving O(1) access; static.
A collection of named fields that may hold different data types, e.g. student.name.
An ordered, immutable sequence whose values cannot be changed after creation, e.g. (x, y).
A dynamic structure of nodes, each holding data plus a pointer to the next node; easy to insert/delete but with no random access.
A LIFO structure with push, pop and peek at the top via a stack pointer; can overflow or underflow.
A FIFO structure where items are enqueued at the rear and dequeued at the front; circular queues reuse space with rear = (rear + 1) MOD size.
A binary tree where the left subtree is less than the node and the right subtree is greater, giving O(log n) search when balanced.
A structure that maps a key to an index using a hash function for ~O(1) lookup; a collision is two keys mapping to the same index.

content.heading: Static vs dynamic structures
points: Static structures have a fixed size set at declaration and occupy a contiguous block of memory, e.g. an array.
Dynamic structures grow and shrink at run time, allocated from the heap as needed, e.g. a linked list.
Trade-off: static gives fast, simple access but may waste or run out of memory; dynamic is flexible but adds pointer/management overhead.

content.heading: Linear structures: arrays, records, lists, tuples
points: Arrays are indexed from 0 and can be 1D, 2D (grid[r][c]) or 3D (cube[l][r][c]), all holding one type.
Records group named fields of differing types; lists are mutable, resizable and may hold mixed types.
Tuples are ordered but immutable, so they suit fixed groupings such as coordinates.

content.heading: Stacks and queues
points: A stack is LIFO: push and pop happen at the top, used for call stacks, undo and backtracking.
A queue is FIFO: enqueue at the rear, dequeue at the front, used for spooling, buffers and scheduling.
A circular queue reuses freed space with rear = (rear + 1) MOD size; a priority queue serves the highest priority first.

content.heading: Linked lists, graphs and trees
points: A linked list uses a head pointer and nodes ending in null; it must be traversed in order with no random access.
Graphs hold vertices and edges (directed/undirected/weighted), stored as an adjacency matrix (dense) or list (sparse).
Trees are connected and acyclic with a root, parents, children and leaves; a binary tree has at most two children per node.

content.heading: BSTs, traversals and hash tables
points: In a BST, insert by going left if smaller and right if larger; in-order traversal returns the values in ascending order.
Pre-order (root, L, R) is used for copying/prefix; post-order (L, R, root) is used for deletion/postfix.
A hash table indexes keys via a hash function (e.g. key MOD size); collisions are resolved by linear probing or chaining, and a higher load factor slows it down.

worked_example.title: Tracing a stack and a queue
steps: Stack (top listed first), starting empty: push(5) -> [5]; push(9) -> [9, 5]; push(2) -> [2, 9, 5].
Stack result: values popped were 2 then 7; final stack top to bottom is 9, 5.
Queue (front listed first), starting empty: enqueue(A) -> [A]; enqueue(B) -> [A, B]; dequeue() returns A -> [B].
enqueue(C) -> [B, C]; enqueue(D) -> [B, C, D]; dequeue() returns B -> [C, D].
Queue result: values dequeued were A then B; final queue front to rear is C, D.
Key point: the stack reverses order (LIFO) while the queue preserves arrival order (FIFO).

task: Insert 50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60 in that order into an empty binary search tree and draw the result, then write the in-order traversal and confirm it is in ascending order.
challenge: Compare an array and a linked list for implementing a list that grows unpredictably: explain the access-time, memory and insertion/deletion trade-offs, and state which you would choose and why.

exit_ticket.q: State the ends used for enqueue and dequeue in a queue.
exit_ticket.a: Enqueue at the rear, dequeue at the front.

exit_ticket.q: Give one difference between a static and a dynamic data structure.
exit_ticket.a: A static structure has a fixed size set at declaration, while a dynamic structure grows and shrinks at run time (allocated from the heap).

exit_ticket.q: Why is a stack the right structure for a browser Back button?
exit_ticket.a: It is LIFO, so the most recently visited page is popped first, returning pages in the correct reverse order.
